speaker: interviewer
text: September 14, 1995-- this is an interview with Morris Libfeld, L-I-B-F-E-L-D. My name is Fran Starr, S-T-A-R-R. I'm the interviewer. We are in the city of Toronto, Canada. And the interview will be conducted in English. Today is Thursday, September 14, 1995. My name is Fran Starr. I will be interviewing Morris Libfeld. We are in the city of Toronto, Canada. And the interview will be conducted in English. Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us your full name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Morris Libfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell your surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-O-R-R-I-S L-I-B-F-E-L-D

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Libfeld, when were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kielce, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us a little bit about your parents? What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents-- my father's name was Alexander. My mother's name was Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a factory from parquet, wood parquet, all his life, his growing up life, because his father, my grandfather, built that factory. They start-- they were working by hand at that time. They didn't even have machines. But my father, when he grew up, he installed all machines. And he had about 70 to 75 people working year-round.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this factory was in Kielce?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory was in Kielce. It was right across our, our house where we lived, the same street, on [? Stolarska. ?] We lived [? Stolarska ?] three. And that was the [? last ?] one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work at the factory as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she never worked at all. My mother was from a, from a family. When she was born, her mother died. And she was raised by a neighbor until she was two or three years old. Later on, father remarried-- rather, my grandfather. And they took the baby home. At previous, she had 10, there were 10 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your mother's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my mother's family. And they all died after birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were in your family? How many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our family, there were six-- four brothers and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name, the oldest, was Helen. My brother next to her was Avraham. There was Meyer. Then I was. And then was my brother Teddy. After him was my sister Devora.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like being raised in such a large family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Life was beautiful. It was, as young children, we went to school, public school, and then to gymnasium, a Jewish gymnasium. And later on, we became like partners to the, to the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: During your years of growing up and going to school, you went to a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to public school for about two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship like between Jews and Gentiles at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The relation was very-- it was a bad relation. Because the, the reason I went out of school, of public school, is because I couldn't fight the people there, the students, anymore. I, one time, I, they beat me up. And I beat up the rest of the other guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: They beat you up for what reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: For no reason. They didn't have to have a reason. And I quit. I said, I'm not going to school anymore. So my father sent me to gymnasium, to Jewish gymnasium. I went to gymnasium until I was about 16 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the population of Kielce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Population was about around between 80,000 and 90,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what percentage of that population were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: The population of Jewish were around 27,000, 28,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Jewish people live amongst the Gentiles, or were you in a separate area of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were, the Jewish people lived mostly in the city. And the city was like 50-50. And around the city, there lived the Gentiles more. The city was a, it was a nice city. In the summertime, we couldn't go swimming because we were beat up by, by the Polacks. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no reason. Because we were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything else that you were restricted from doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to quit the school. And I had to, I, I couldn't go out, like a picnic or anything else out of the city because we were in danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this an accepted way of life? Did people talk about the anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, it was accepted after 19-- 1928, I think. Because I don't remember exactly the date. But when Marshal [POLISH] Moscicki president of Poland, when he died, at that time started up the anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of activities did you enjoy as a boy growing up? Did you play any sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I played the sports. At school, we had all kind of sports. Played the soccer, basketball. We were skiing and skating. But as far sports, we had to do it, because we were, we were all in the city. We didn't go out of city. So we weren't, it was a big part of our city was Jewish. So we weren't that, that much afraid for ourselves that we're going to be harmed or anything. It's going to happen. Like to our, our street where we lived and we had the factory, we, there was no Polacks coming in there, because they were afraid to come in. If they did anything wrong before, they got beat up from the guys around in the, in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were beat up by whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] boys. We took revenge, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often would these incidences happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: How often? Not too often, but I would say once or twice a year. Because Gentile people never came into our, our street. On the whole street was one Gentile living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever consider leaving Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was considering leaving Poland in 1933, but it didn't materialize so just gave it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reason was he considering leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was going to leave just a short before the war, my oldest brother. And my father didn't let him go, because he had, he was religious. And he had friends which went to America and came back. And they were telling that there's no Jewishness and so on. So he didn't let any of his children go. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we all stayed at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your goals as a young boy growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: My goal? Oh, I knew what's going to happen with me. I would, I would go in as a partner to the factory and work with, with my brothers. That was, was always talked about. Before the war, I, I went to work because we did a lot of-- most of our work was done outside on buildings, like in all Poland. And we used to travel. My father used to travel when he was well. But in 19-- what was it-- 32, I think, he went to [POLISH]. And he was in a bus accident. He had to be operated right way, because he flew with his head through the window. And it cut around his head completely. You could lift up his skin over from the head all the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had to go. There was no hospital in Kielce, so they should make the operation. But we had a doctor, which he was the chief doctor in the army. So my father knew him well. So he said, if you can get a-- from the hospital, from the military hospital, if you can see that you get him in, I'll do the operation on him. So my brother, I think, or my sister, they went to the Red Cross. And through the Red Cross, we got the OK that he's going. But we had to pay 10,000 zloties to the Red Cross. So they let them in for operation, into the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that doctor operated on him. And he was all right after, a few, a few months. But in the time being, when he was laid up, my brother took over all the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother, Avraham.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did life change for you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: When life changed? It was in, in 1939, when the war break out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What changes happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What changes? For one thing, the Germans came and took our factory away. It took them about six months to take out the merchandise, the, the parquet and all kind of machineries from the factory. My father was-- he knew a guy which he was a [NON-ENGLISH]. And he called on him. And he made some arrangements that he should leave a couple machines at the factory. It cost him at that time 20, 20,000 zloties. But after, after a while, after I don't know, I went, I personal went in '39, in the end of '39, I went to Russia, I, myself, because I didn't want to stay in, at home, not because I had to work or anything. I was talking like that at the time. Everybody was talking about what's going to happen. But believing or not believing, I went with a friend of mine. And we walked from Kielce to Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends, [? Manella. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you do when you arrived at Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, when we arrived to Lublin, we, to coming into Lublin, we had to go through a bridge. The bridge was knocked out during the fighting. So we had to walk over to the other side to reach the other side. We walked over, and the German, the [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know if you ever heard of [NON-ENGLISH,] They took us in and they made two rows of [NON-ENGLISH] And we had to go through in the middle. And they had sticks and, and rubbers, and all kind of-- they were hitting us. And we, we went through that, that that, we can say like, it was a hell. So we came through the other side. We went out to the, the street. They took away everything what we had with us. And we were left with nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had with me some clothes. And I had some tools, which from home, I took some tools. Not too many, because I had to carry everything. So when we went through the Jewish community there, they came down. And they took us out, took us out whatever, whoever was, we went about 12 boys. We went to one, one family. It was a rich family before the war. They had a hardware store. And we were there for about two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. You know, he had a big warehouse with a hardware warehouse. And after this, they contacted some Polacks. And they took us over on the other side of the, of the river. We came on the other side of the river. We had to walk, I don't know, 10 or 15 miles. The, the river was a shallow river, but it was at a time when it wasn't frozen. And it wasn't-- it was just the top a little bit frozen. And we thought that it's frozen. We walked through. We fell into the water. And, A and we came out of there. The Russians were guarding the river. But we had, you know, from hearing from people which were going back and forth, they told us that if they catch you, if the Russians catch you, you just say that you want to go to Poland, not to Russia, just to Poland. If you wanted to go to Poland, they took you back to Russia. So that's what had happened. I came there. I went to Rovno. In Rovno, I had there a cousin, which is now in Israel, and his grandfather and grandmother and uncles and aunts, the whole family, from [POLISH] Poland. They, they he lived there. He was a young kid. He went to school over there. And then way down when the war got-- when the Russians pulled back, they went to deep Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Rovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there about six weeks. And I couldn't stand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I, I was eaten up by life, from lice and all kind of-- so I decided to leave everything and go back home. I came back home. And it was six weeks later. I took my whole wardrobe, whatever I had. It was freezing cold. And I, I left everything on the snow. And I went up to the house. So I got new clothes and new, everything new.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after this, my, my father run out of funds. So my brother and my second, my other brother, they went to work. And they were earning some money, you know, that wasn't enough to make a living. Because when I went to Russia, I took some money with me, because I had my own money, which I earned from working before the war. And-- When you returned back home to Kielce.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I returned back, I went to work. I earned some money. And I gave it to the, to my mother. This way, she, you know, made, we made a living for not long, for about two months. After two months, we had a friend which his daughter worked by the German. And she came back with a offer we should make army beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remind myself. I don't, I don't recall. Her name was-- I don't recall now what her name was. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe it will come back later. But my father accepted their price. And he renovated some the, the factories, so we can get work and started to work those beds. We got contracts from the government at 59 mark a bed. But we made a lot of money at that time, at this kind of money. We worked for the Germans all through 1942. We had 150 people working, like every shift was 150 people. We had three shifts a day. But they didn't have no mechanic. And I was the only mechanic which could handle all the machines. So I worked for six weeks without stop, day and night. When you came in the morning, by 6:00 o'clock in the morning, I fell asleep on the, on the sawdust.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, your family were still living in the family home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were still at home until 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened in 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: In between, the ghetto started up, but we were in the ghetto. So we didn't have to, to move out. In 1942, they liquidated the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Another thing, they liquidated in three shifts, like three days within six days. We went the first day. So we, everybody went. My, my brother and my sisters, they went with the first shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all went to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know at the time where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time where they were going, we didn't know. We on--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know of--

speaker: interviewee
text: We only found out about three days later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We found out that they were came to Treblinka, and they were liquidated there on the second day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find this out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found it out through friends of mine. I remember one name, Monek Medlah. They, before they came to in, into the camp on the railroad train, they told them to undress. And they left all their clothes and their luggage and everything on the train. Now between the train, some people, like two or three friends, I think, they hid underneath the clothes. And when the train went back, on the way, they jumped the train. And they came back home. When they came back, they told the whole story. So three days later, we knew everything what goes on.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll go to break now.
